question: Compare the NuScale thermal power per module from Ch.01 with the DHRS initial power assumption from Ch.05.

nuscale_ch01_0626.title: DSRS 14.2, Rev 0: Initial Plant Test Program - Design Certification and New COL applicantsII.4 Initial Startup Tests
nuscale_ch01_0626.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for initial plant test programs, specifically addressing the applicability and level of conformance with DSRS 14.2 and SRP 14.3 requirements for design certification and combined license (COL) applicants. It notes which subheadings and items are applicable to each applicant type and indicates whether the criteria are fully, partially, or not applicable, with some requirements only relevant to COL applicants or specific amendment requests.

nuscale_ch01_0810.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.4 Detect and Suppress Method: Exclusion zone and buffer region methodology
nuscale_ch01_0810.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals are designed to prevent conditions that could lead to unstable flow oscillations. Instead, the existing reactor trip system (RTS) provides protection by tripping the reactor before safety limits are violated, making additional detect and suppress (D&S) methods unnecessary. Backup options and stability-related instrumentation for D&S are not applicable, as the RTS already ensures compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale reactor pressurizer, including low alloy steel for the baffle plate, stainless steel and nickel-based alloys for cladding, heater bundles, cover plates, and bolting, all in accordance with relevant BPVC specifications. It also references where additional material descriptions can be found in other report sections.

nuscale_ch01_0811.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.5 Detect and Suppress Method Trip of reactor before SAFDL violation
nuscale_ch01_0811.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability, as existing reactor trip signals already prevent violation of safety limits before unstable flow oscillations can develop. The design partially conforms to some DSRS 15.9.A requirements by ensuring reactor trips occur before SAFDL violations, but does not employ detect and suppress systems, as stability is managed through existing trip signals rather than direct detection and suppression of instabilities. Backup options and additional monitoring systems are deemed not applicable due to the effectiveness of the current reactor trip system.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and decay heat from the reactor core during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch01_0766.title: DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System Malfunction That Increases Reactor Coolant InventoryII.4.A Input Parameters and Initial Conditions - Initial Power Level
nuscale_ch01_0766.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for chemical and volume control system malfunctions that increase reactor coolant inventory. It states that NuScale meets the requirements for initial conditions such as power level, scram characteristics, and core burnup, and clarifies that certain guidance for pressurizer relief valves does not apply to NuScale’s design, as it uses spring-loaded safety valves instead of power-operated relief valves. Any potential mechanical failure is addressed in a separate analysis section.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references compliance with RG 1.105 and relevant sections of the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch01_0108.title: 8.37 ALARA Levels for Effluents from Materials Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0108.summary: This section provides a legend for electrical symbols and notations used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, specifically for single-line diagrams of materials facilities. It details the meanings of various transformer, breaker, battery, and other electrical component symbols, including their ratings and configurations, to ensure accurate interpretation of the facility’s electrical schematics.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring efficient operation and safety margins, with the system designed for low pressure losses to support natural circulation.

nuscale_ch01_0018.title: Principal Design Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0018.summary: The section describes the principal design criteria of the NuScale plant, focusing on the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) and the overall facility process. It outlines the reactor core’s heat transfer process, natural coolant circulation, steam generation, and the flow of superheated steam to the turbine and condenser, emphasizing the plant’s cooling and power generation mechanisms.

nuscale_ch01_0109.title: Figure 1.7-1a: Electrical Symbols
nuscale_ch01_0109.summary: This section provides a legend for electrical symbols used in NuScale’s single-line diagrams, detailing the meaning of each symbol and associated notations for components such as transformers, circuit breakers, batteries, fuses, and switches. It explains how to interpret ratings, quantities, and other key parameters indicated on the diagrams to ensure accurate understanding of the electrical system layout.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational events such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, event classification, and instrument setpoints, ensuring plant safety and compliance with the Standard Review Plan (SRP) and Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS). No deviations or exceptions are noted.

nuscale_ch01_0005.title: Reactor Type
nuscale_ch01_0005.summary: The NuScale reactor is a small modular pressurized water reactor (PWR) featuring a cylindrical steel containment vessel designed to ASME standards, which operates under vacuum during normal conditions and does not require insulation between the reactor and containment vessel. Each NuScale Power Module (NPM) integrates the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel, uses natural circulation for cooling (eliminating the need for pumps), and is rated at 160 MWt (about 50 MWe), with up to 12 modules per plant for a total net output of approximately 570 MWe.

nuscale_ch01_0671.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Analytical ParametersInitial Power Level is 102%
nuscale_ch01_0671.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, which address transients such as decrease in feedwater temperature, increase in feedwater or steam flow, and inadvertent operation of turbine bypass or decay heat removal systems. The analysis confirms that the initial power level, conservative scram assumptions, and core burnup parameters all conform to requirements, with no limiting single failure identified. The referenced table indicates overall conformance with the applicable sections of the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing real-time pressure and level data to control systems.

nuscale_ch01_0120.title: 1.8.2 Departures
nuscale_ch01_0120.summary: Section 1.8.2 describes the requirement for a Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification to provide a list of any departures from the certified design. This ensures that any differences between the certified design and the site-specific implementation are clearly identified and addressed in the licensing process.

nuscale_ch01_0073.title: Departures
nuscale_ch01_0073.summary: This section outlines the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified portions and COL items, which are site-specific responsibilities. Additionally, it specifies that COL applicants must provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0652.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.8.B Sequence of Events and Systems Operation
nuscale_ch01_0652.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, noting areas of full and partial compliance with DSRS 15.0 requirements. Specifically, for Section I.8.B on Sequence of Events and Systems Operation, NuScale partially conforms, with the exception of Item B.vi, which applies only to COL applicants. The table outlines how NuScale addresses each criterion, highlighting any deviations or stricter standards used.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest in the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related support systems and backup power equipment. It also highlights human factors considerations, emphasizing fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management to minimize human error and meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0004.title: Containment Type
nuscale_ch01_0004.summary: The NuScale containment vessel is a cylindrical, steel, ASME-certified pressure vessel designed to withstand high-pressure events and operates under vacuum during normal conditions, eliminating the need for insulation. The reactor is a passive, integral small modular pressurized water reactor (SMR) with natural circulation and no external piping for steam generators or pressurizer, and each module produces about 50 MWe, with a 12-module plant yielding approximately 570 MWe net output.

nuscale_ch01_0651.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.8.A Identification of Causes and Frequency Classification
nuscale_ch01_0651.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, noting areas of full and partial compliance with DSRS 15.0 requirements. While most criteria are met, some aspects—such as frequency analysis for AOOs and specific acceptance criteria—only partially conform due to differences in methodology or applicability. The identification of causes and frequency classification is fully addressed, with exceptions noted for items specific to COL applicants.

nuscale_ch01_0664.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Basic ObjectiveIdentify Limiting Increase in Heat Removal Events
nuscale_ch01_0664.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for analyzing limiting increase in heat removal events, such as decreases in feedwater temperature, increases in feedwater or steam flow, and inadvertent operation of turbine bypass or decay heat removal systems. It confirms that the NuScale design meets the objectives of identifying limiting events and verifying that fuel damage and system pressure criteria are satisfied, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0008.title: Format and Content
nuscale_ch01_0008.summary: The section describes the NuScale containment vessel as a cylindrical, ASME-certified steel vessel designed to withstand high-pressure events, with internal vacuum pressure during normal operations. The NuScale reactor is a passive, integral small modular pressurized water reactor that eliminates the need for external piping and reactor coolant pumps, with each module producing about 50 MWe and a 12-module plant yielding a net output of approximately 570 MWe. The section also notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide their own project schedules.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, core design, and safety systems, showing that NuScale uses significantly smaller modules, simplified systems, and different safety components compared to conventional PWRs. The comparison is summarized in tables for clarity.

edges.description: Section A provides the initial plant test program context, which includes verifying the NuScale module's thermal power as described in Ch.01, while Section B discusses the thermal hydraulic stability and the need for reactor trip signals based on initial power assumptions as used in Ch.05; thus, the thermal power value from Section A is a prerequisite input for the initial power assumption in Section B's safety analysis.

Section A provides the thermal power per module from Ch.01, which serves as the initial condition for the DHRS initial power assumption described in Section B from Ch.05, connecting the design value to the system's safety analysis input.
Section A provides the tested thermal power per module value from Ch.01, which serves as the basis for the initial power assumption used in the DHRS analysis in Section B from Ch.05.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone method and instead relies on reactor trip signals to prevent CHF violations, while Section B specifies that the reactor is tripped before SAFDL violation, both describing protective actions that are causally linked to thermal power assumptions and safety limits.

Section A provides information on the NuScale module's thermal hydraulic stability and reactor trip protection, which determines the maximum thermal power per module, while Section B describes the DHRS's design basis, which assumes an initial power level (thermal load) that must be removed after reactor trip; thus, the thermal power per module from Section A sets the initial condition for the DHRS power assumption in Section B.

Section A discusses the NuScale design's approach to thermal hydraulic stability and reactor trip signals, while Section B specifies the initial power level used as an input parameter for safety analysis; the initial power value from Section B is a prerequisite for evaluating the effectiveness of the protective actions described in Section A.

Section B's DHRS design basis assumes an initial power level that should correspond to the thermal power per module specified in Section A, establishing a connection where the design input in B is derived from the design value in A.

Section A provides the thermal power per module from Ch.01, which serves as the initial condition for the DHRS power removal assumption described in Section B from Ch.05, connecting the design value to the system's safety analysis basis.
Section B's DHRS initial power assumption relies on the thermal power per module value specified in Section A, establishing the initial condition for accident analysis.

Section A describes the DHRS's function to remove decay heat based on initial operating conditions, which are determined by the module's thermal power as specified in Section B's initial power level input parameters.
Section A provides the thermal power per module design value from Ch.01, which is used as an input or assumption for the DHRS initial power condition analyzed in Section B from Ch.05.
Section A provides the NuScale module's thermal power design value from Ch.01, which is used as an input assumption for the DHRS initial power analysis in Section B from Ch.05.

Section A discusses the establishment of steady state power levels for the NuScale module, which provides the design thermal power value that is used as an input or assumption for the DHRS initial power condition referenced in Section B's initial startup test program.

Section A discusses the establishment of steady state power parameters for the NuScale module, which are necessary inputs for the DHRS initial power assumption referenced in Section B's regulatory review context.
Section A references Section B by directing the reader to Section 5.4 for further description of the steam generators (SGs), which are relevant to the DHRS initial power assumption.

Section A discusses system pressure criteria during operational events, which depend on the thermal power per module from Ch.01, while Section B outlines initial startup tests that must verify assumptions such as the DHRS initial power from Ch.05; thus, the thermal power value from Ch.01 is a prerequisite for setting and validating the DHRS initial power assumption in Ch.05.

Section A provides information about the NuScale module's thermal power as part of the system response to operational transients, which is a key input or assumption for the DHRS initial power condition analyzed in Section B's thermal hydraulic stability review.

Section A discusses system pressure criteria and operational events relevant to the NuScale module's thermal power, while Section B details the design and materials of the pressurizer, which is a component affected by and designed to accommodate the thermal power and pressure conditions described in Section A.

Section B establishes steady state parameter values for the DHRS as a function of reactor power, which is specified in Section A as the NuScale thermal power per module, thus connecting the DHRS initial power assumption to the design value from Ch.01.

Section A discusses specific operational events and criteria related to the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which depend on the initial thermal power per module described in Section B's general plant and reactor core overview.

Section A and Section B both reference the same DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4 criteria and regulatory conformance statements, with Section A including additional details about instrument spans and setpoints, thus Section A provides further specification for the general regulatory criteria described in Section B.

Section A discusses the establishment of steady state power parameters for the NuScale module, which are necessary inputs for the DHRS initial power assumption referenced in Section B's analysis of system response to operational events.

Section A provides the initial power level assumption (102%) used in DHRS analyses, which must be consistent with the thermal power per module described in Section B's initial startup test program to ensure test conditions reflect design and safety analysis assumptions.

Section A provides the initial power level (102%) used in accident analyses, which is a key input or assumption that must be consistent with the DHRS initial power assumption referenced in Section B for thermal hydraulic stability evaluations.

Section A discusses the analytical parameter of initial power level (102%) used in DHRS-related safety analyses, which should be based on or compared to the NuScale module's thermal power as described in Section B, establishing whether the DHRS assumption aligns with the actual design value.
Section B provides references to other sections that describe the materials and components mentioned in Section A's general description of the pressurizer.

Section A describes the interface requirements and boundaries for the NuScale certified design, which includes the thermal power per module, while Section B outlines the initial startup test program requirements, which would use the certified thermal power value as an input assumption for testing the DHRS initial power.

Section A provides the certified design parameters, including the NuScale module's thermal power, which must be used as an input or boundary condition for the DHRS initial power assumption analyzed in Section B.

Section A outlines the interface requirements and boundaries for the NuScale certified design, which would include the thermal power per module, while Section B details the design of the pressurizer and its materials, which are relevant to the DHRS initial power assumption as they affect heat removal capabilities.

Section A provides the certified design parameters, including the thermal power per module, which serve as the basis for the initial power assumptions used in the DHRS startup tests described in Section B.

Section B assumes an initial power level of 102% for DHRS analysis, which should be based on or compared to the actual NuScale module thermal power described in Section A, connecting the design value to the analytical assumption.

Section A describes the containment vessel design and its compliance with ASME codes, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and boundaries between the certified design and site-specific design, with both sections connected by their focus on defining and bounding the certified design elements relevant to regulatory compliance.

Section A describes the containment vessel design and its compliance with ASME code, while Section B outlines the interface requirements between the certified design and site-specific design, with both sections referencing the scope and boundaries of the certified design as defined in Section 1.2.
Section A specifies the initial power level (102%) used in DHRS event analyses, which must be based on the NuScale module's thermal power as described in Section B's reactor system design.

Section A discusses the analytical parameter of initial power level (102%) used in DHRS-related transients, which should be based on or compared to the certified module thermal power described in Section B's design interface documentation.

Section A discusses the analytical parameter of initial power level (102%) relevant to DHRS operation, while Section B outlines the certified design interfaces, which include the thermal power per module as a design value referenced in Ch.01; thus, the initial power assumption in Section A is based on the design value specified in Section B.

Section A describes the pressurizer's design and function within the reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and boundaries between the certified NuScale design and site-specific elements, with both sections connected by their roles in defining and bounding the certified reactor module's systems relevant to power assumptions.

Section A describes the pressurizer design within the reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines the interface requirements between the certified design (including the reactor module) and site-specific elements, connecting them through the identification of certified system boundaries relevant to power assumptions.

Section A provides the design basis thermal power per NuScale module, which is the reference value used in Section B's initial startup tests and DHRS initial power assumption to ensure test conditions reflect actual operating parameters.

Section A provides general information about the NuScale module's thermal power, which is a key input for the DHRS initial power assumption discussed in Section B, as the DHRS analysis relies on the module's rated thermal power to establish initial conditions.

Section A provides the regulatory context for classifying and analyzing events such as AOOs, which informs the assumptions and scenarios (including initial power levels) that must be validated by the initial startup tests described in Section B.

Section A provides the design and safety analysis context, including thermal power values for the NuScale module, which are necessary for the initial power assumptions used in the DHRS startup tests described in Section B.
Section A provides the NuScale module's thermal power design value, which is the basis for the DHRS initial power assumption referenced in Section B's thermal hydraulic stability analysis.

Section A discusses the objectives and regulatory criteria for analyzing limiting heat removal events, which depend on the module's thermal power as a key input, while Section B details the reactor coolant system and pressurizer design, which must accommodate the thermal power levels established in Section A.
Section A provides the nominal thermal power per NuScale module, which is the design value that should be used as the initial power assumption in the DHRS startup tests referenced in Section B.

Section A provides the nominal thermal power per NuScale module, which is the design value that should be compared to the DHRS initial power assumption referenced in Section B to determine if the assumption is consistent with the actual module design.
Section A provides the nominal thermal power per NuScale module, which is the design value that should be used as the initial power assumption in the DHRS analysis referenced in Section B.

Section A and Section B both discuss the categorization and classification of events (such as AOOs) in the context of transient and accident analyses, referencing the same regulatory criteria and partially conforming to frequency-based classification requirements.

The DHRS initial power assumption in Section B is based on the NuScale thermal power per module value provided in Section A, as the assumed initial power for accident analysis must reflect the actual design thermal power.

Section A discusses the categorization and analysis of transient and accident events, which relies on design parameters such as the module's thermal power specified in Section B's description of the containment vessel's design basis.
Section B provides the nominal thermal power per NuScale module, which is the design value that should be used as the initial power assumption in DHRS analyses referenced in Section A.

Section A provides the general classification and regulatory context for transient and accident analyses, while Section B specifies the objectives and criteria for analyzing specific heat removal events, both relying on the NuScale module's thermal power as a key input parameter connecting the two.
Section B provides the nominal thermal power per NuScale module, which is the design value that should be used as the initial power assumption in the DHRS analysis referenced in Section A.

Section A discusses the regulatory objectives and accident analysis criteria, which require knowledge of the NuScale module's thermal power (from Section B) to establish initial conditions such as the DHRS initial power assumption.

Section B provides the nominal thermal power per NuScale module (in Table 1.3-1), which is the design value referenced or used as an initial condition or assumption in Section A's DHRS initial power assumption for accident analysis.
